name: camellia sinensis
mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Catechins, notably EGCG, and caffeine are highlighted in relation to modest body-weight effects; catechins are also implicated in green-tea-extract hepatotoxicity, with significant product variability.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: Beverage is generally safe, but extracts may cause nausea, constipation, abdominal discomfort, or increased blood pressure; liver injury reports primarily involve extract products.
description: Green, black, and oolong teas come from Camellia sinensis but differ by processing; green tea leaves are steamed/pan-fried/dried and not fermented.
dosage: unresolved for oral supplement dosing; FDA-approved sinecatechins 15% ointment is applied three times daily, but that is topical drug dosing, not supplement dosing.

interactions: High-dose green tea reduces nadolol blood levels; green tea extract reduces atorvastatin blood levels; interaction described with raloxifene.
preparation: Beverage and dietary supplements/extracts; processing differs from black/oolong tea.
region: Native range is E. Himalaya to S. China and N. Indo-China, Hainan.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for mild digestive comfort
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: Green tea beverage is generally not associated with major safety concerns in adults, but concentrated green tea extracts can cause GI side effects and are linked to uncommon cases of clinically apparent liver injury, including rare severe outcomes. High-dose green tea can reduce blood levels of nadolol and atorvastatin; an interaction with raloxifene is also described.
